title: 'The Walking Dead: Dead City' Returns With Action-Packed Season 3 Trailer

blocks.content: Caroline Preece has more than 15 years of experience writing entertainment content. Her work for outlets including Den of Geek, Backstage, Tom’s Guide, CultBox, and Starburst prioritizes sharp analysis and reader-first reporting with a particular interest in the ways storytelling, audience behavior, and digital culture drive modern entertainment trends.

The Walking Dead has spent the better part of two decades finding new corners of its apocalypse to pick over, and most of those spin-offs have worn their bleak pessimism on their sleeve. The latest trailer from one of them does something a little different, trading some of the usual dread for the odd comedy of two old enemies trying to build a working city out of a walker-infested ruin.

The spin-off centers on two former characters from the original series, dropped into a walled-off Manhattan overrun by the dead and forced to survive each other as much as the walkers. Two seasons in, that feud has been the whole engine, which is what makes the new trailer's lighter register such a surprise, right down to a bartender working the apocalypse for laughs.

AMC has released the first full-length trailer for The Walking Dead: Dead City's third season ahead of a July 26 return, bringing back Jeffrey Dean Morgan and Lauren Cohan as Negan and Maggie, the franchise's longest-running grudge match. AMC's synopsis has the pair finally setting aside their differences to build the first thriving community in Manhattan since the apocalypse, only for fresh chaos to test whether either of them has actually changed.

The trailer opens with Maggie calling New York "the last best hope for any of us" over a shot of the Statue of Liberty dissolving into a walker taking a knife to the skull — a pretty concise mission statement. The bigger surprise in the new footage is tone. Whereas the first two seasons traded almost entirely in misery (something carried over from its parent show), this one lets some air in, most obviously through Jimmi Simpson, who turns up as a Manhattan bartender claiming he can talk to the undead. It's a supporting turn that could potentially walk off with the season, and it sits alongside a needle-drop of "New York Groove."

Simpson joins a refreshed roster that also includes Raúl Castillo, Aimee Garcia, and Michael Emery, with Logan Kim back as Maggie's now-teenaged son Hershel, a part brought over from the original series. Seth Hoffman returns as showrunner, and Scott M. Gimple still oversees the wider universe, which AMC continues to expand through long-floated crossover plans. The new season will run eight episodes, so fans will have plenty to sink their teeth into.

Dead City has had an unusually stop-start life. The first season landed in 2023, the second was held up by that year's writers' strike and didn't arrive until 2025, and AMC has already confirmed that the eventual fourth season will be the show's last, news that followed close behind the Season 3 renewal. Season 2 tied off its central threat with the Dama's death, leaving Maggie and Negan to decide, in the finale, whether they could move forward together.

The trailer seems happy to leave that question hanging. Negan and Maggie agreed to keep going as allies, yet the ghost of Glenn still sits between them, and this franchise has never met a truce it couldn't tear apart due to fear or self-interest.
